channel.title: NYT > Technology
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Tech Workers Maxed Out Their A.I. Use. Now They’re Trying to Minimize It.
item.description: Artificial intelligence is expensive to use, many companies discovered. That has led to a new era of saving costs.
item.dc:creator: Eli Tan
item.media:credit: Andrea Chronopoulos

item.title: New Super PAC, the Guardrails Alliance, Aims to Rally Tech Workers to Help Limit A.I.
item.description: The Guardrails Alliance, which has raised $5 million, is positioning itself as a populist effort that will take on the pro-A.I. interests trying to influence this year’s elections.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac and Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shaunna Thomas is a founder of the Guardrails Alliance, a new super PAC trying to take on moneyed entities that oppose reining in artificial intelligence.

item.title: Trump Says Apple to Buy Computer Chips from Intel
item.description: While neither company has publicly discussed the deal, it would be a big break for Intel, which sold a 10 percent stake to the U.S. government last year.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Intel employee holding a silicon wafer at the company’s Chandler, Ariz., fabrication plant.

item.title: Amazon Retaliated Against Workers Who Supported Regulating Data Centers, Complaint Says
item.description: The employees encouraged limits on the complexes in a series of hearings in the tech giant’s hometown, Seattle.
item.dc:creator: Karen Weise
item.media:credit: Jovelle Tamayo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Amazon’s campus in Seattle, Wash.

item.title: Have You Bought a Tesla Recently? We Want to Hear From You.
item.description: Tesla sales are rebounding in Europe even though many people have negative feelings about Elon Musk, the company’s chief executive. We want to know why.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:credit: Nam Y. Huh/Associated Press
item.media:description: Tesla’s car sales took a big hit in Europe last year, in part because many people were turned off by the political views of the company’s chief executive, Elon Musk.

item.title: As India Temporarily Bans Telegram, Which Other Countries Have Restricted the Messaging App?
item.description: Several countries have restricted the messaging app, a valuable platform for dissidents, criminals and extremists. Here’s an overview.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:credit: Sasha Maslov for The New York Times

item.title: Elon Musk’s Next Move May Be a Mega-Merger of SpaceX and Tesla
item.description: Some shareholders might object, but there is little they could do, legal experts say.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing and Ryan Mac
item.media:credit: Mike Blake/Reuters
item.media:description: Tesla has sold car and batteries to SpaceX, and the two companies are jointly working on chips and software.

item.title: How the ‘Hard Fork’ Hosts Bring Their Tech Podcast to a Live Audience
item.description: Kevin Roose and Casey Newton explain why they try to include “a little chaos” when they record their New York Times tech podcast in front of a crowd in San Francisco.
item.dc:creator: Kami Rieck, Kevin Roose and Casey Newton
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left: Microsoft’s chief executive, Satya Nadella, was interviewed by Kevin Roose and Casey Newton for “Hard Fork Live” last week at the Blue Shield of California Theater in San Francisco.

item.title: Anthropic Employees Accuse Trump Administration of Targeting Them
item.description: Workers at the artificial intelligence company have been puzzled and increasingly concerned by the administration’s move to limit their latest A.I. models.
item.dc:creator: Sheera Frenkel, Julian E. Barnes and Dustin Volz
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Jason Henry/Bloomberg
item.media:description: Anthropic’s headquarters in San Francisco, where some employees are wondering if they are being picked on by President Trump.

item.title: Why Waymo’s Driverless Taxis Won’t Be on Your Streets Anytime Soon
item.description: The Silicon Valley company’s ambitions to roll out autonomous cars nationwide have hit political roadblocks in some of the country’s biggest markets.
item.dc:creator: David McCabe
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Hard Fork’ Live Part 2: Dylan Field on Standing Out in the A.I. Era
item.description: “I think if you have a creative voice in writing or design, you put yourself out there and you take a risk — this is a good time to do that,” said the Figma chief executive Dylan Field.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Roose, Casey Newton, Whitney Jones, Rachel Cohn, Vjeran Pavic, Alyssa Moxley, Dan Powell, Elisheba Ittoop, Marion Lozano, Rowan Niemisto and Diane Wong
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times. Photo: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times

item.title: The Cloud Has Sound: The Unrelenting and Unseen Cost of A.I. Data Centers
item.description: As tech giants rush to build infrastructure, some residents who live near data centers say a constant low-frequency vibration is ruining their health and homes.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Danielle Parhizkaran/The Boston Globe, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A 350,000-square-foot data center in Lowell, Mass., is abutted by homes. The site was once the Lowell Bleachery and Dye Works, and then for six decades the Prince Spaghetti factory.

item.title: D.O.J. Seeks to Halt Pollution Lawsuit Against Elon Musk’s Data Center
item.description: The department cited national security concerns, saying Elon Musk’s company had played a crucial role in the Iran war. It also argued it has the authority to stop environmental lawsuits brought by citizens.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: National Assn for the Advancement of Colored People
item.media:credit: Brad J. Vest for The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction at xAI’s power plant in Southaven, Miss., in February. The NAACP has sued the company over air pollution from generators there.

item.title: Riding High After I.P.O., SpaceX Will Buy A.I. Start-Up for $60 Billion
item.description: SpaceX exercised its option to acquire Cursor in an all-stock deal, bolstering Elon Musk’s ambitions in artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac and Gregory Schmidt
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times
item.media:description: The deal to acquire Cursor signals how Elon Musk is delving further into A.I. and expanding SpaceX from its main businesses of building rocket ships and offering satellite internet service.

item.title: A.I. Boom Ignites Asian Chip Companies
item.description: They make much of the gear that goes into giant data centers. Demand for their products is shifting the balance of tech power.
item.dc:creator: Paul Mozur and Tripp Mickle
item.media:credit: I-Hwa Cheng/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Nvidia’s chief executive, Jensen Huang, signing a Taiwanese bank note at the Computex technology conference in Taipei, Taiwan, this month.

item.title: What to Know About Planned Social Media Bans in Australia, the U.K. and Beyond
item.description: Britain said it would ban social media access for children under 16 starting in 2027, joining several other countries introducing similar measures.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:credit: Katie Collins/Reuters
item.media:description: Students in Wimbledon, London, during an interview this year about social media. Public opinion in many countries has coalesced around the idea that more must be done to protect children online.

item.title: UK Announces Social Media Ban for Children Under 16
item.description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer said his government planned to bar children under 16 from social media, following similar efforts in Australia and elsewhere.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:credit: Katie Collins/Reuters
item.media:description: High school students in Wimbledon, London, this year during an interview about social media. Britain plans to place an age limit on social media.

item.title: Merger Cleared, David Ellison to Join Trump at U.F.C. Bouts
item.description: A U.F.C. fight on President Trump’s birthday will now double as a capstone for Paramount’s successful effort to secure Justice Department approval for a mega media merger.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: The specially constructed Claw on the South Lawn of the White House, where the Ultimate Fighting Championship bouts will be held on Sunday.

item.title: State Attorneys General Are Investigating OpenAI
item.description: OpenAI said that a coalition of states had opened an investigation over a wide range of its practices, including its handling of user data, safety of minors and advertising activities.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang and David McCabe
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: “We take the concerns raised by state attorneys general seriously,” OpenAI, headed by Sam Altman, said in a statement on Saturday.

item.title: U.S. Bars Foreigners From Using Anthropic’s Most Advanced A.I. Models
item.description: The company said on Friday night that the federal government had ordered limits on its Mythos and Fable 5 A.I. systems, citing national security concerns.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Dustin Volz
item.media:credit: Nicolas Tucat/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Anthropic’s Mythos A.I. model has raised worries that it could be used to attack computer networks.

item.title: SpaceX’s Unlikely Journey From Far-Out Idea to $2 Trillion Juggernaut
item.description: Elon Musk said he had initially given SpaceX less than a 10 percent chance of succeeding. His rocket company has come a long way.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac, Kenneth Chang and Kirsten Grind
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times
item.media:description: A SpaceX ad on the Nasdaq MarketSite tower in Times Square. The company, which Elon Musk founded in 2002, began trading on the stock market on Friday.

item.title: What the SpaceX I.P.O. Means for OpenAI and Anthropic
item.description: Strong investor interest in SpaceX could be good news for the A.I. rivals, which have signaled that they, too, intend to go public this year.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: If You Have a 401(k), How Much SpaceX Stock Will You Own?
item.description: If you have index funds — such as in a 401(k) — they may soon include shares of the mega company. Here’s a rough calculation.
item.dc:creator: C.J. Robinson
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Elon Musk Becomes World’s First Trillionaire as SpaceX Stock Begins Trading
item.description: With SpaceX shares soaring 20 percent on their first day of trading, the world’s richest person crossed another milestone — one with 13 digits.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac and Ben Casselman
item.media:credit: Adrees Latif/Reuters
item.media:description: Elon Musk, the chief executive of SpaceX, in 2022 at the company’s facility in Brownsville, Texas.

item.title: SpaceX Stock Rises 11% in Largest IPO Ever
item.description: The blockbuster stock market debut of Elon Musk’s rocket company paves the way for a wave of enormous offerings that are coming from OpenAI and Anthropic.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Traders at the New York Stock Exchange watching news about SpaceX’s initial public offering on the Nasdaq exchange on Friday.

item.title: ‘Hard Fork’ Live, Part 1: Satya Nadella and Cindy Cohn
item.description: A tech titan, a digital rights defender and two menacing-looking robot dogs took the stage at our second-ever live show.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Roose, Casey Newton, Rachel Cohn, Whitney Jones, Vjeran Pavic, Alyssa Moxley, Dan Powell, Elisheba Ittoop, Marion Lozano, Rowan Niemisto and Diane Wong
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times. Photos: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times

item.title: Google Sues to Stop Chinese Cybercrime Group from Using Its A.I.
item.description: In a lawsuit, the tech giant accused the group of using Google’s Gemini system to create hundreds of fake corporate and government websites.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Andria Lo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Google warned that artificial intelligence had supercharged the problem of online scams.
